items.myth: Matplotlib and Seaborn are competing libraries—you must choose one.
items.reality: Seaborn is built on Matplotlib; it generates Figure and Axes objects you can further customize with ax.set_* and fig.savefig. Learning Matplotlib makes Seaborn easier, not redundant.
items.example: sns.boxplot(...) returns an Axes; call ax.set_title('Validation F1 by fold') afterward for report-ready text.

items.reality: show() displays interactively (when a GUI backend exists) but does not write files; persistence requires fig.savefig with an explicit path and format.
items.example: A notebook cell that only calls plt.show() leaves no PNG on disk for a LaTeX paper—add fig.savefig('fig1.pdf').

items.myth: The default figsize is fine for slides and papers.
items.reality: Default (~6.4×4.8 inches) often yields tiny text when scaled; set figsize and dpi at creation time for the target medium.
items.example: fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6)) before plotting so axis labels stay legible in a projected slide.

items.myth: Matplotlib owns embedding interpretation and confusion-matrix semantics.
items.reality: Matplotlib provides drawing primitives (scatter, imshow); embedding visualization owns coordinate-plot interpretation, and the confusion matrix topic owns metric construction and normalization.
items.example: ax.imshow(cm) draws pixels; whether cm is row-normalized and what off-diagonal cells mean belongs elsewhere.
